lessons.title: Coroutine Mechanics: co_yield, co_return, co_await
lessons.motivation: Before C++20, the only way to write a lazy generator was to store state manually: a class with counter fields, a `next()` method, and a "finished or not" flag. This is verbose and fragile. Coroutines change the picture entirely: a function can **suspend** in the middle of execution, return a value to the caller, and **resume** later — keeping all its local variables intact. This opens the door to lazy sequences (Python-style generators), asynchronous code without callback hell, and data-processing pipelines with natural control flow.
lessons.theory:
## What is a coroutine A coroutine is a function that can **suspend** and **resume**. Unlike a regular function that either runs to completion or throws an exception, a coroutine can "freeze" its state and hand control back to the caller, then continue from the same point later. Difference from `std::thread`: a thread runs concurrently on another core, while a coroutine runs cooperatively on the same thread. There is no synchronization overhead or data races by design. **A function is a coroutine** if its body contains at least one of three keywords: `co_yield`, `co_return`, or `co_await`. ## co_yield — suspend and produce a value ```cpp co_yield expr; ``` Suspends the coroutine and delivers the value `expr` to the calling code. The caller receives the value, does something with it, then calls `resume()` — and the coroutine continues from the point right after `co_yield`. Example: a generator of numbers from 1 to N: ```cpp Generator range(int n) { for (int i = 1; i <= n; ++i) co_yield i; // suspend + produce } ``` ## co_return — complete the coroutine ```cpp co_return; // for void coroutines co_return expr; // for coroutines returning a value ``` `co_return` terminates the coroutine. After it, `coroutine_handle::done()` returns `true`. If a coroutine has no explicit `co_return`, the compiler inserts one implicitly at the end of the function (analogous to `return;` in a `void` function). ## co_await — suspend until an awaitable is ready ```cpp co_await expr; ``` `co_await` suspends the coroutine until the awaitable object `expr` signals "I am ready". Once the awaitable is ready, the coroutine resumes. This is precisely how asynchronous code is structured: instead of callbacks you write linear code, and the compiler splits it into pieces. ## Coroutine frame — local variables on the heap When a coroutine function is called, the compiler allocates a **coroutine frame** on the heap. This frame stores: - all local variables of the coroutine, - the current execution point (where it is suspended), - the `promise_type` object (see below). This is why local variables of a coroutine remain alive between `resume()` calls — they live not on the stack (which would be destroyed when the function returns) but on the heap. ## std::coroutine_handle\ — handle for managing a coroutine `std::coroutine_handle

` is a lightweight pointer to a coroutine frame. Through it you can: - `handle.resume()` — resume the coroutine's execution, - `handle.done()` — check whether the coroutine has finished, - `handle.destroy()` — free the coroutine frame (must be called exactly once). The handle is obtained from `promise_type` via `coroutine_handle

::from_promise(promise)`. ## std::suspend_always and std::suspend_never — basic awaitables C++20 ships two simplest awaitable types: | Type | Behavior | |---|---| | `std::suspend_always` | always suspends | | `std::suspend_never` | never suspends | They are used in `promise_type` to control behavior at startup and completion: - `initial_suspend()` — what to do when the coroutine starts: returning `suspend_always` → lazy coroutine, which must be explicitly `resume`d; returning `suspend_never` → eager coroutine, starts immediately. - `final_suspend()` — what to do on completion: almost always `suspend_always`, so we have a chance to read the final state before `destroy()`. They can also be used directly in the coroutine body with `co_await`: `co_await std::suspend_always{}` — an explicit suspension point; `co_await std::suspend_never{}` — a point that does not suspend. ## Why C++20 has no ready-made coroutine types C++20 defines the **mechanism** of coroutines (three keywords + `coroutine_handle` + `suspend_always`/`suspend_never`) but ships no ready-made wrapper types like `Generator` or `Task`. This is intentional: the mechanism is low-level enough for library authors to implement any desired semantics. The standard `std::generator` only arrives in C++23. In lessons m4-l2 and m4-l3 we will write our own Generator and Task from scratch to understand how everything works under the hood.

examples.title: First coroutine: Generator<T> and co_yield
examples.explanation: Generator<T> is a minimal wrapper over the coroutine machinery. In `promise_type`, the method `yield_value` is called by the compiler at each `co_yield`: it stores the value and returns `suspend_always`, suspending the coroutine. The calling code reads the value via `gen.value()` and resumes via `gen.next()` (which calls `handle.resume()`). The infinite `fibonacci()` is safe: the coroutine suspends after each `co_yield`, and we take exactly 8 values.

examples.title: co_await: suspend_always vs suspend_never (lazy and eager startup)
examples.expectedOutput:
--- Lazy ---
coroutine created, not started yet
lazy: step 1
after first resume
lazy: step 2
lazy: step 3
done: 1
--- Eager ---
before creating eager coroutine
eager: running immediately
after eager coroutine
examples.explanation: `LazyTask` uses `initial_suspend() → suspend_always`: when `lazy_coroutine()` is called, the coroutine is created but immediately suspended. Execution only begins on the first `resume()`. `EagerTask` uses `initial_suspend() → suspend_never`: when `eager_coroutine()` is called, the coroutine starts immediately. `co_await std::suspend_always{}` is an explicit pause point inside the body; `co_await std::suspend_never{}` is a point that does not suspend (useful when logic selects an awaitable dynamically). `done: 1` means that after the second `resume()` the coroutine reached `final_suspend` and completed.

examples.title: Coroutine frame: locals survive across suspensions
examples.expectedOutput:
Locals in coroutine frame survive suspensions:
  item_0 (mod#1)
  item_1 (mod#2)
  item_2 (mod#3)

done() tracks coroutine state:
start:         done=0
after yield 1: done=0
after finish:  done=1
examples.explanation: `prefix` and `modification_count` are ordinary local variables, but the compiler places them in the **coroutine frame** (heap-allocated when the coroutine is called). Between `co_yield` points the call stack returns to the caller while the frame stays alive — so `modification_count` correctly increments from 1 to 3. `done()` returns `false` while the coroutine is suspended after a `co_yield`, and `true` only after it reaches `final_suspend` — that is, fully completes.
